rating_source: Amazon UK (aggregate across current paperback and board editions)
rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread — a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question — so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.
include_rationale: This is still the cleanest fit when your nearly-three wants to pick and explain on every page. If a nursery or grandparent copy is already doing the rounds, borrow it first — buy only once you know it isn't already on the shelf.

product_name: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus! (board book)
rank_rationale: #2 over #3: Pigeon's comic call-and-response (say no, explain why) is a stronger nightly talk-back trigger than Oh No George's gentler what-next question, and it has deeper UK review history.
include_rationale: Every spread gives your child a job: say no, explain why, and hold their ground when the pigeon argues back. It's less choice-led than You Choose, but brilliant when they want to talk back with confidence rather than just pick an option.

rank_rationale: #3 over #4: George's repeated what-will-he-do-next beat keeps talk-back going page after page, edging out Shh's quieter suspense which leans more on prediction than explanation.
include_rationale: George keeps asking your child what he should do next, and whether he'll get it wrong again — a proper prediction loop, not a one-off question. Good for a child who likes calling out the answer before you turn the page.

product_name: Shh! We Have a Plan (board book)
rank_rationale: #4 over #5: Shh's silent-plan suspense still asks the child to predict and explain, which sits closer to the cluster's talk-back objective than Shark in the Park's simpler spot-the-shark refrain.
include_rationale: Your child has to work out whether the plan will actually work, and explain what went wrong when it doesn't. Quieter than Pigeon or George, so it's a better fit for a calmer chat rather than a giggly one.

rank_rationale: #5: Included as the lightest, most repeatable option — a good entry point for what-do-you-see chat when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime, even though it asks less explanation than ranks 1-4.
include_rationale: The lightest, most repeatable pick on this list — perfect for a tired evening when the other four feel too talky. Your child answers the same question in a new way each time, which is a gentle entry point into talk-back rather than the deepest one.

rank_rationale: #1 over #2: asks the child to pick and explain on every spread, a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question.
include_rationale: Choice-led pages are the category benchmark for nearly-three talk-back — borrow first if already owned.
exclude_rationale: Very widely owned — check the shelf first.

rank_rationale: #8: ahead of You Choose Bedtime because its dilemmas are more explanation-led than a bedtime-only spin-off of the book already ranked #1.
